metadata.title: The Vienna 4G/5G Drive-Test Dataset
metadata.description:
Vienna 4G/5G Drive-Test Dataset is a large-scale open dataset of cellular measurements collected across Vienna, Austria. It combines passive scanner measurements with active smartphone (UE) logs, providing complementary network-side and user-side views of deployed Long Term Evolution (LTE) and 5G New Radio (5G NR) radio access networks. All measurements are georeferenced (WGS84/EPSG:4326) and cover diverse urban and suburban environments, enabling spatially resolved studies of radio propagation, coverage, and performance.

In addition to measurements, the dataset provides inferred deployment descriptors for a representative subset of cells, including estimated site locations, sector orientations, and antenna heights derived from measurement-based inference (e.g., timing-advance–based localization and signal-strength analysis). LTE measurements can be associated with inferred deployment metadata via standardized network-reported identifiers. For 5G NR handset measurements collected in non-standalone (NSA) mode, where physical NR identifiers are not exposed, the dataset includes auxiliary dataset-internal identifiers (cell_id_dummy, gnb_id_dummy) where reliable estimates are available.

To support environment-aware analysis and digital-twin workflows, the repository also includes a building and terrain model of Vienna released by the City of Vienna under CC BY 4.0 (see city_model/README.md for attribution and details). Together, the measurement data, inferred deployment descriptors, and environmental context enable geometry-conditioned and environment-aware machine learning tasks (e.g., radio map estimation, propagation modeling, and digital-twin calibration), as well as benchmarking for coverage analysis and base station localization.

Contents (high level)

  • Scanner measurements (LTE & 5G NR): snapshot-based detections with per-cell metrics; CINR is used only for LTE scanner, while other quality ratios are reported as SINR.

  • Smartphone/UE logs (LTE & 5G NR NSA): connected-mode measurements with radio, mobility, and QoS-relevant KPIs.

  • Estimated cell information: inferred positions, orientations, and antenna heights (subset coverage).

  • City model (optional): building and terrain rasters for Vienna (separately documented).

License

  • Measurement data, inferred deployment descriptors, and repository documentation: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0).

  • Building and terrain model: CC BY 4.0, attributed to Stadtvermessung Wien / Open Government Data Wien (see city_model/README.md).

Citation

Please cite the dataset DOI and the associated journal paper:

  • Dataset (all versions): https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18322065

  • Paper: See preprint identifiers in "Related Works"

metadata.title: IOTC Resolution 23/01: On the management of anchored fish aggregating devices (AFADs)
metadata.description:
Description

This record provides the official text of IOTC Resolution 23/01 (2023), which establishes management requirements for anchored Fish Aggregating Devices (AFADs) used in fisheries targeting tuna and tuna-like species in the IOTC area of competence.

Key provisions:

  • Requires CPCs to develop and implement AFAD management plans, including deployment, monitoring, and retrieval practices
  • Establishes mandatory marking of AFADs with unique identification numbers and reporting of deployment data
  • Introduces obligations to maintain registers of deployed, lost, and abandoned AFADs
  • Requires the use of non-entangling materials and encourages the use of biodegradable components in AFAD design.

creators.name: Indian Ocean Tuna Commission
creators.affiliation: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations - Fisheries and Aquaculture Division

data.title: IOTC Resolution 23/01: On the management of anchored fish aggregating devices (AFADs)

McCluskey, occupied mine on Betula (iNat 121440807); NEWFOUNDLAND AND LABRADOR: Division No. 5, NF, 49.345878, −57.176048, 15.vii.2022, empty mines on Betula papyrifera (iNat 128779952); NOVA SCOTIA: Cumberland Co., 45.679052, −64.060999, 13.vi.2022, benkendrick, occupied mine on Betula populifolia (iNat 121704764); Lunenburg Co., New Germany, 44.533750, −64.701172, 2.vi.2024, M. Zifkin, occupied mines on Betula pumila (iNat 219988327); PRINCE EDWARD ISLAND: Queens Co., 46.240475, −62.802263, 21.vi.2021, R.W. Harding, empty mine on Betula populifolia (iNat 83990922).

Hosts. Betulaceae: [Betula alleghaniensis Britton], B. *lenta L., B. *papyrifera Marshall, [B. pendula Roth, B. pendula ssp. mandshurica (Regel) Ashburner & McAll., B. populifolia Marshall, B. pumila L.]. Also see Comments.

Leaf mine. Long, gradually widening, linear, on the upper surface; initially whitish with the fine frass grains in two neat rows, later more or less suffused with brown (or greenish, in the mines on B. lenta) and with the frass scattered at random (illustrated in Eiseman (2025, p. 1280)).

Puparium. Brown; formed outside the mine.

Phenology and voltinism. Our specimens were collected as larvae in early June and early October, in both cases emerging as adults the following spring (Fig. 1). We have found mines already empty on 31 May, and occupied mines have also been found in early September.

Distribution. * USA: [AK], MA, [ME, MN, NH, NY, OH, PA, VT; Canada: AB (Spencer 1969a), BC, NB, NL, NS, ON (Spencer 1969a), PE, QC (Spencer 1969a)]; widespread in Europe, and also recorded from northwestern Russia (Nartshuk & von Tschirnhaus 2015) and Iran (Dousti 2010). The Canadian records of Spencer (1969a, p. 62) are listed under “Unidentified Leaf-mines of Agromyza species,” with the note, “This exactly resembles the mine of Agromyza alnibetulae Hendel and may well represent this species but no adults have been encountered among the hundreds of specimens examined.”

Comments. Agromyza alnibetulae was originally described from Austrian material reared from Betula pendula Roth and Alnus incana (L.) Moench, and Hering (1957) stated that this species also occurs rarely on Carpinus L. (Betulaceae). Spencer (1969b) determined that all Alnus -associated flies represented a distinct species, which he described as Agromyza alnivora Spencer. Spencer (1990) never commented on the identity of the Carpinus feeder. We have seen linear mines of an unknown agromyzid—possibly A. alnivora —on Alnus across northern North America, but no adults have yet been reared from these. Agromyzid mines have not been found on Carpinus in North America.

An existing illustration of the male phallus of Agromyza alnibetulae was provided in Spencer (1969b, 1972), wherein a single aspect of the phallus was given. This figure was reproduced in subsequent publications until recently, but it is not entirely representative of the typical morphology of the species (B. Warrington, pers. comm.). As such, the genitalia of the newly collected male, representing one of the first two known non-Palearctic specimens, are illustrated here (Figs. 218–224).

metadata.title: China Offshore Wind Turbine SAR Dataset (COWT-SAR)
metadata.description:
The COWT‑SAR dataset provides a vectorized inventory of offshore wind turbines (OWTs) across China’s coastal waters for the period 2020–2025. Derived from Sentinel‑1 SAR imagery using the structure‑aware deep learning framework SA‑OWTDet, the dataset includes 8,593 turbines with attributes such as geographic location, installation year, distance to shore, water depth, and associated sea area.

This dataset supports the consistent identification, mapping, and monitoring of offshore wind energy infrastructure, and facilitates applications in energy system analysis, marine spatial planning, infrastructure management, and a wide range of geospatial studies.

data.title: China Offshore Wind Turbine SAR Dataset (COWT-SAR)
